title: South Korea Readies First Rate Hike in Three Years as Middle East Risk Spreads, Trump Slaps 20% Toll on Hormuz
subhead: The Bank of Korea is set to raise rates Thursday as South Korea declares a Middle East emergency framework, while Trump's unprecedented 20% Hormuz transit fee and full Iranian port blockade push oil above $85 and force Asian economies to build war-level supply chain defenses.

sections.heading: Bank of Korea Poised for First Rate Hike Since 2023
sections.body:
South Korea's central bank is expected to raise its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 2.75% on Thursday, according to a Reuters poll showing 36 of 37 economists forecasting the move. It would be the BOK's first increase in more than three years. A second hike to 3.00% is anticipated by year-end.

The driver is persistent inflation: consumer prices hit 3.2% in June, the highest in two and a half years and well above the BOK's 2% target for four consecutive months. Rising oil prices from the renewed US-Iran conflict and a weakening won — down more than 4% against the dollar this year — are adding to price pressures. Economists expect inflation to average around 3% in the second half, according to the Reuters survey.

The move follows rate hikes by central banks in Australia, New Zealand, Indonesia, and the Philippines, making South Korea the latest Asian economy to shift toward tighter policy as the Middle East conflict feeds through to domestic prices. Bank of America economist Benson Wu noted policymakers will closely monitor currency weakness, though the bank does not currently expect consecutive rate hikes.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea is the first major Asian economy to explicitly tie a rate decision to Middle East-driven inflation risk. If the won continues to weaken and oil stays elevated, the BOK's forecast of 3% inflation for H2 could prove optimistic, forcing more aggressive tightening than markets currently price.

sections.heading: South Korea Declares Middle East Emergency Framework
sections.body:
At a Cabinet meeting on Tuesday, Deputy Prime Minister Koo Yun-cheol briefed President Lee Jae Myung on an emergency governance framework spanning prices, energy, employment, and finance in response to the renewed US-Iran conflict and Iran's declaration of a Strait of Hormuz closure.

The government raised the risk level for vessels transiting the strait to 'Serious,' the highest tier. Seoul said it has secured more than 100% of average prior-year volume for crude oil imports scheduled for July and August, and enough naphtha to meet July demand. Domestic gasoline and diesel prices have fallen to the 1,800-won-per-liter range after a reduction in the maximum price ceiling.

But the government acknowledged that a prolonged oil price rise could increase pressure on consumer prices, corporate production costs, employment, and financial markets. It plans to announce a 'won internationalization roadmap' this month to promote won-denominated transactions, and is building a 'K-supply chain' encompassing domestic production, expanded stockpiling, overseas production bases, and import source diversification. South Korea's current account surplus reached a record $38.61 billion in May, with the cumulative January-May total of $141.28 billion already surpassing 2025's full-year figure.
sections.whyItMatters: Seoul's response is the most comprehensive national-level financial defense mounted by an Asian economy against the Hormuz disruption — combining price controls, supply chain diversification, and currency internationalization. The won internationalization push, in particular, signals that Middle East risk is accelerating dedollarization hedging in export-driven economies.

sections.heading: Trump Imposes 20% Hormuz Transit Toll, Blockades Iranian Coastline
sections.body:
President Trump announced the US would act as the 'guardian' of the Strait of Hormuz and impose a 20% fee on all cargo transiting the waterway to cover American security operations. He named Gulf states that should reimburse the US for protection costs. The US military separately announced it will reinstate a full naval blockade of Iranian ports beginning July 14 at 2000 GMT.

Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi responded sarcastically on social media, saying Iran has 'always been the GUARDIAN of the Strait' and that '20% is of course too much. We will be fair.' The International Maritime Organization, the UN's shipping regulator, told Reuters there is no legal basis for imposing mandatory tolls on transit through an international strait.

UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer called for a restriction-free Strait of Hormuz, with critics branding the toll 'economic extortion.' An Australian former diplomat warned of 'worst-case scenario' impacts on Australian fuel imports.

The toll and blockade came on the third consecutive night of US strikes on Iran. CENTCOM confirmed strikes on Bushehr, Bandar Abbas, Chah Bahar, Jask, Konarak, and Abu Musa, targeting coastal defense systems, missile and drone sites, and maritime capabilities. The US also used sea drones in combat for the first time, striking a submarine and ship maintenance facility at Bandar Abbas. Iran targeted two UAE-flagged tankers in the strait, killing one Indian crew member and injuring eight others.
sections.whyItMatters: The 20% Hormuz toll is an entirely new form of financial warfare — monetizing a military chokepoint. It has no precedent in international maritime law and no clear enforcement mechanism, but the announcement alone is enough to reshape shipping economics. If even partially implemented, it adds a direct cost layer on top of war-risk insurance premiums already pricing in the conflict.

sections.heading: Oil Surges Above $85 as Blockade Replaces Ceasefire
sections.body:
Brent crude surged more than 9% on Monday to above $85 per barrel — a one-month high — after the US announced the Iranian port blockade and the Hormuz transit toll. The spike marks the largest single-day gain since April, repricing the entire market's assumption that the June ceasefire would hold.

Indian markets opened under pressure on Tuesday, with analysts noting that retail inflation hit an 18-month high while crude traded at a one-month high. India's reliance on Hormuz-transiting crude makes it especially exposed: the country imported a record 4.5 billion euros worth of Russian crude in June, according to local media, a 34% increase from the previous month, as refineries accelerated purchases ahead of potential disruption.

The Economist noted that 'deal or no deal, oil prices will stay high for months,' reflecting a market consensus that even a renewed ceasefire would leave a significant risk premium baked into prices through the end of 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil above $85 with no ceasefire in sight means the inflation impulse from the Iran conflict is now feeding into central bank rate decisions globally — from the BOK this week to the Fed and ECB later this month. India's record Russian crude imports show how secondary sanctions waivers (issued in March to calm markets) are reshaping trade flows in real time.

sections.heading: Japan's Katayama Opens Door to GPIF Portfolio Review
sections.body:
Japan's Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said Tuesday that the Government Pension Investment Fund — the world's largest pension fund with roughly $1.8 trillion in assets — could reconsider its portfolio allocation if investing conditions change materially. She cited government initiatives to boost the nation's growth as enhancing the attractiveness of Japanese assets.

The comments came days after she said the government was exploring ways to encourage pension funds to invest more in domestic instruments, a signal that sent 10-year JGB yields sharply lower and helped the yen recover last week. The 10-year JGB yield was last at 2.775%, with the yen at 162.40 against the dollar.

Separately, Reuters reported that Japan has no immediate plans to change target asset allocations of its state pension fund, and that any pivot in its massive retirement assets would likely manifest as a 'slow redirection of maturing debt rather than a fire sale,' according to sources familiar with the thinking.

The GPIF reviews its allocation every five years, with the current equal-split plan across domestic bonds, domestic stocks, foreign bonds, and foreign stocks having taken effect in April 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: Even a small shift by the $1.8T GPIF would move global bond and equity markets. Katayama's comments signal the Japanese government is actively pushing for more domestic investment — a potential tailwind for JGBs and Japanese equities, and a headwind for the global flows that have been supporting foreign asset prices. The timing, amid a weak yen and rising global rates, makes this a signal worth watching.

sections.heading: SBI Launches Yen Stablecoin Lending, Partners with Solana for Onchain Finance
sections.body:
SBI VC Trade announced a JPYSC stablecoin lending service launching July 16, offering a 3% annualized yield on 12-week loans — exceeding the 0.325%-1% rate SBI cited for ordinary yen deposits. The product is the first to allow Japanese customers to lend yen-denominated stablecoins for passive yield, marking a step from payments infrastructure to yield-bearing financial instruments.

Separately, SBI Holdings announced a strategic partnership with the Solana Foundation to build a Japanese onchain financial market. The Solana Foundation will buy into SBI R3 Japan, to be renamed SBI Solana Global, with a focus on expanding the yen-backed stablecoin and tokenized real-world assets across Asia. The initiative targets institutional onchain financial services, cross-border payments, and payment infrastructure for AI agents.

These moves follow Japan's April 2026 amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act to classify crypto assets as financial instruments, and Prime Minister Takaichi's recent reaffirmation of support for Web3 startups including increased government-backed funding.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is building regulated onchain yen infrastructure at speed — from legal reclassification of crypto assets to stablecoin yield products to blockchain partnerships. The SBI-Solana alliance positions Japan as a potential hub for tokenized finance in Asia, with JPYSC as a vehicle that could compete with dollar stablecoins in regional settlement.

sections.heading: Singapore GDP Tops Estimates, AI Manufacturing Offsets Middle East Headwinds
sections.body:
Singapore's economy grew 5.7% year-on-year in Q2 2026, exceeding market expectations of 5.5%, according to advance estimates from the Ministry of Trade and Industry. Manufacturing was the key driver, accelerating to 10.4% growth from 8.4% in Q1, powered by AI-related demand in electronics and precision engineering.

Not all sectors shared the momentum. Chemicals and biomedical manufacturing faced headwinds from supply chain disruptions and Middle East-linked geopolitical tensions. Services growth slowed to 4.6% from 6.2%, and construction cooled to 6.2% from 12.9%.

Singapore's May exports surged 38% driven by AI-related demand, according to Nikkei Asia, underscoring how concentrated global AI investment has become in a small number of trade-dependent economies.
sections.whyItMatters: Singapore is the clearest case study of a two-speed economy: AI-driven manufacturing roaring ahead while traditional trade routes face Middle East disruption. The 38% export surge shows where global capital is flowing, but the chemicals and biomedical slowdown is a reminder that Hormuz-linked supply chain risk hits even the most diversified Asian hubs.

bottomLine: Today's developments show the Iran conflict reshaping monetary policy in real time: South Korea is hiking rates Thursday specifically because Middle East-driven inflation is pushing past its target, while simultaneously building the most comprehensive financial defense framework of any Asian economy. Trump's 20% Hormuz toll — legally dubious but economically potent — adds a new cost layer to global energy trade on top of war-risk insurance already surging. Meanwhile, Japan is sending contradictory signals: the BOJ rate hike to a 31-year high coexists with the finance minister nudging the world's largest pension fund toward domestic assets, and the SBI-Solana alliance is quietly building yen-based onchain financial infrastructure. Oil above $85 means the inflation impulse is now feeding into every central bank's next decision.
sources: Reuters, EconoTimes, News18, CBS News, Manila Standard, Morningstar/Dow Jones, Cointelegraph, SBI Holdings press release, Nikkei Asia, The Economist, India Today, Channel News Asia, Herald Business Korea, Bloomberg, MSN
